g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of the Multiplexer, a fundamental building block in digital electronics and computer organization.
g.text: A Fundamental Digital Circuit for Data Routing and Selection

g.data-narration: A multiplexer routes one of many input signals to a single output based on selection lines, acting like a digitally controlled switch.
g.text: A combinational circuit that selects one of many inputs and forwards it to a single output.
Often called a "data selector" - acts like a digitally controlled multi-position switch.

g.data-narration: A MUX has three main components: data inputs (2^n), selection/address lines (n), and a single output line.
The 2:1 MUX selects between two inputs based on one selection bit - output follows I0 when S=0, I1 when S=1.
The 2:1 MUX circuit uses NOT, AND, and OR gates to implement the Boolean expression Y = S̄·I₀ + S·I₁.
A 4:1 MUX uses 2 selection lines to choose between 4 inputs. S1S0 = 00 selects I0, 01 selects I1, and so on.
The Boolean expression for 4:1 MUX sums all minterms where each input is selected based on selection line combinations.
MUX size scales exponentially - 8:1 needs 3 select lines, 16:1 needs 4 select lines, following the 2^n pattern.
Multiplexers can implement any Boolean function by connecting function outputs to MUX inputs, with variables on select lines.
g.text: A MUX can directly implement any Boolean function - connect variables to select lines, function outputs to data inputs.

g.data-narration: Multiplexers enable data routing in CPUs, parallel-to-serial conversion, memory addressing, and efficient Boolean function implementation.
In summary, a MUX selects one input from 2^n based on n selection lines, outputting the chosen data to a single line.
g.text: Function: Select one of 2ⁿ inputs → single output
Control: n selection lines choose which input passes through
Nature: Combinational circuit (output depends on current inputs)

g.data-narration: The multiplexer is an essential digital workhorse, enabling efficient data selection in CPUs, communication systems, and memory designs.
g.text: Fundamental to computer organization, data routing, and Boolean function implementation
Master multiplexers to understand how CPUs efficiently manage and route data
